insight: Early Prismata players, including top-10 ranked players, had significantly mistaken strategic beliefs: they considered Iso Kronus overpowered, practiced 'omega clicking' (buying Omega Splitter excessively), and top player elyot reportedly did not consider 4RR 10-supply Frost Brooder to be overpowered. These opinions are now considered laughable by modern standards.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about the unreliability of early-era Prismata player opinions when evaluating original Deadeye Operative balance.

insight: mrguy888 asserts that Endotherm Kit is the only truly unplayable unit in Prismata, implying all other units have at least situational viability.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about Thermite Core balance, where the question of unplayable units came up.

insight: The current (post-patch) Thermite Core is more than twice as strong as the old version because it hides up to 8 damage instead of 4, and because the scaling value of hidden damage increases non-linearly — more hideable damage means each individual Thermite Core is more threatening for longer. Additionally, the old version required owning extra Blastforges, which is a cost if you don't want those Forges later.
author: mrguy888
context: Community discussion evaluating whether Thermite Core is overpowered and comparing old vs. new versions.

insight: The old Thermite Core was primarily only useful for buying a single copy using a spare blue, hoping to exploit opponent granularity issues, because the opponent starts overdefended from starting Engineers and an absorber purchase, so the Pixies generated would not produce meaningful threat value before the Thermites ran out.
author: awaclus
context: Expert discussion comparing old and new Thermite Core designs.

insight: In Prismata, 'first anything gets an edge' is a general principle — the relevant strategic question is always whether the tempo gained from being first to purchase a key unit (e.g., Tarsier, Cynestra, Omega Splitter) outweighs the value of giving the opponent first absorb.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about whether Bombarder is player-favoring due to first-buyer advantages.

insight: Going DD D BBA to get Bombarder on turn 4 is not optimal play, and the community agrees that 'first Bombarder' is not what wins — implying Bombarder's strength comes from integrating it correctly into an economy rather than rushing it at all costs.
author: amalloy
context: Debate about whether Bombarder is player-favoring because of first-buyer advantages, analogous to Odin.

insight: A gold cost nerf to Bombarder would be more effective at addressing the problem of players massing Bombarders after buying only a couple Tarsiers, compared to a supply nerf. The 5th Bombarder is rarely relevant in either the mirror or non-mirror matchup, so supply reduction would not address the core issue.
author: mrguy888
context: Community debate about how to nerf Bombarder — gold cost vs. supply reduction.

insight: Prismata is fundamentally an unfair game: with extremely rare draw exceptions (from specific units), every set is a forced win for one player or another at perfect play. In practice, skill determines outcomes because the game is too complex to solve.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about draws in Prismata and whether 0% draw rate is a design problem.

insight: A community test measuring set player-favoredness found that the most accurate predictors among experienced players estimated favoredness at around 55–60% on average, while actual P2 winrate was around 52%. This suggests many sets are meaningfully player-favoring at the level of skilled play, though the test was noisy due to rating imbalance between players and only 20 rounds of data.
author: apooche
context: Discussion about whether a 50/50 overall winrate implies player-favoredness has been eliminated in Prismata.

insight: In high-economy sets with Doomed Drone present, P2 can open ED1 / DDD to gain a medium economy suited to building and protecting an Amporilla, while P1 does not have a clearly dominant response. The DD/DDE opening is considered especially strong for P2 in this context.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about whether Doomed Drone is P2-favoring in high-economy sets.

insight: In blue-first high-economy sets with Doomed Drone, P1 can respond with DD DDE DDD DDDB, which is a more convenient path to blue tech than the alternative D1E DDD D11B.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about P1 responses to P2's strong Doomed Drone openings in high-econ sets.

insight: Militia costs 3B plus a Drone (i.e., 1GD in effective resource terms), not a flat gold amount — making it significantly more efficient at times when a player wants to be selling Drones anyway.
author: amalloy
context: Correction of a player who mis-stated Militia's cost as 4G, clarifying its actual cost structure and strategic context.

insight: Soaking a Doomed Mech with remaining lifespan (not at 1 or 2 lifespan) in order to purchase a new Doomed Mech is almost never correct play.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of a specific in-game decision involving whether to absorb with a Doomed Mech to buy a replacement.

insight: When using Apollo to snipe blockers, targeting Conduits is a high-value play. However, if Zemora Voidbringer is also present, players must prioritize sniping Zemora (not Wall or other units) because Zemora will continue firing every other turn regardless of other snipes, quickly negating the value of Apollo's action.
author: zera777
context: Player recounting a mistake where they used Apollo to snipe a Wall instead of Zemora Voidbringer, leaving Zemora active.
